sourceMovie.title: Roofman
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Roofman works as a crime movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The crime and comedy blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Old Man & the Gun
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Old Man & the Gun (2018) is the kind of film Roofman fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The crime overlap is real, but it's the funny emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: The Sugarland Express
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Sugarland Express shares Roofman's gift for crime — the 1974 film delivers comedy without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: Panic Room
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Roofman compelling — the crime, the drama — shows up in Panic Room (2002) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Naked Gun 33⅓: The Final Insult
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The crime energy of Roofman is alive in Naked Gun 33⅓: The Final Insult (1994), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a funny payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: A Perfect World
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: A Perfect World (1993) operates in the same crime as Roofman, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Bonnie and Clyde
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Bonnie and Clyde (1967) belongs to the same conversation as Roofman — both anchor their crime in drama emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: The Iceman
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Roofman's crime stayed with you, The Iceman (2012) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The drama through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Bandits
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Roofman excels in crime, Bandits (2001) doubles down on comedy to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: It Could Happen to You
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: It Could Happen to You (1994) is the natural next step after Roofman: same comedy DNA, different perspective. The funny streak and drama elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Where the Heart Is
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Roofman who respond to its comedy will find Where the Heart Is (2000) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

faq.question: What should I watch after Roofman?
faq.answer: Try The Old Man & the Gun, The Sugarland Express, Panic Room—each shares DNA with Roofman while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Roofman?
faq.answer: Roofman (2025) is best described as Crime, Comedy, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Roofman worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.1/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
